questions.promptTex: \text{A } 12\text{-foot ladder leans against a wall, making a } 70° \text{ angle} \\ \text{with the ground. How high up the wall does it reach?} \\ \text{(Round to 2 decimals; use } \sin 70° \approx 0.940 \text{)}
hints: Sketch: ladder = hypotenuse, wall height = opposite, angle is at the base.
steps: Sketch a right triangle: ladder is the hypotenuse, wall is the vertical leg, ground is the horizontal leg. The $70°$ angle is between the ladder and the ground.
Identify what's known/unknown: hypotenuse $= 12$, want the **opposite** side (wall height $h$).

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 15\text{-foot ladder leans against a wall. Its base is } 5 \text{ feet} \\ \text{from the wall. What angle does the ladder make with the ground} \\ \text{(round to nearest 0.1°)?}
hints: Ground distance is the adjacent side, ladder is the hypotenuse.
steps: Sketch: ladder $= 15$ (hyp), ground distance $= 5$ (adjacent to the angle of inclination).
Two sides involved: adjacent + hypotenuse → use **cosine**.

questions.promptTex: \text{A ladder reaches } 8 \text{ feet up a wall and makes a } 60° \text{ angle} \\ \text{with the ground. How long is the ladder?} \\ \text{(Round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Wall height = opposite = 8. Ladder = hypotenuse = unknown.

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 20\text{-foot ladder is placed against a building. The base must be} \\ 4 \text{ feet from the building (safety code). How high up the building} \\ \text{does the ladder reach? (Round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Two sides known (ladder + ground), no angle. Use the Pythagorean theorem!
steps: Identify: ladder $= 20$ (hyp), ground distance $= 4$ (adj), want wall height (opp). No angle is given — and you don't need one.

questions.promptTex: \text{A ladder rests against a wall at an angle of } 75° \text{ with the ground.} \\ \text{The base of the ladder is } 3 \text{ feet from the wall.} \\ \text{How long is the ladder? (Use } \cos 75° \approx 0.259 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Ground distance = adjacent = 3, ladder = hypotenuse = unknown.
steps: Identify: angle $= 75°$, ground distance $= 3$ (adjacent), want ladder (hypotenuse).

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 25\text{-foot ladder leans against a wall, reaching } 24 \text{ feet up. } \\ \text{What angle does the ladder make with the ground (round to nearest 0.1°)?}
steps: Identify: ladder $= 25$ (hyp), wall height $= 24$ (opposite), want the angle with the ground.
(This is the larger acute angle of a $7$–$24$–$25$ Pythagorean triple.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A guy wire is attached to the top of a } 24\text{-foot pole and to a stake} \\ 7 \text{ feet from the base of the pole. How long is the wire?}
hints: Right triangle with legs $24$ and $7$ — wire is the hypotenuse.
steps: Sketch: pole vertical (length $24$), ground horizontal (length $7$), wire from the top of the pole to the stake — that's the hypotenuse.
Two legs known, no angle → Pythagorean theorem: $$L^2 = 24^2 + 7^2$$
(This is the $7$-$24$-$25$ Pythagorean triple — worth recognizing.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A guy wire makes a } 65° \text{ angle with the ground and is anchored} \\ 12 \text{ feet from the base of a tower. How tall is the attachment point} \\ \text{on the tower? (Use } \tan 65° \approx 2.145 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Pole height = opposite = unknown. Ground distance = adjacent = 12.
Two legs of the right triangle → use tangent: height $= 12 \cdot \tan 65°$.
steps: Identify: angle $= 65°$ (with ground), ground distance $= 12$ (adjacent), want pole height (opposite).

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 50\text{-foot guy wire is attached to a tower at a height of} \\ 40 \text{ feet. What angle does the wire make with the ground} \\ \text{(round to nearest 0.1°)?}
steps: Identify: wire (hyp) $= 50$, height (opp) $= 40$, want the angle with the ground.
(This is the $3$-$4$-$5$ triangle — its angle opposite the leg of length $4$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A guy wire makes a } 50° \text{ angle with the ground and reaches } 18 \text{ feet} \\ \text{up a pole. How long is the wire? (Use } \sin 50° \approx 0.766 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Pole height = opposite = 18, wire = hypotenuse = unknown.
steps: Solve for $L$: multiply both sides by $L$, then divide by $\sin 50°$: $$L = \dfrac{18}{\sin 50°}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 100\text{-foot tall radio antenna has a guy wire that makes a } 60° \\ \text{angle with the ground. The wire is attached to the top of the antenna.} \\ \text{How far from the base of the antenna is the wire anchored} \\ \text{(round to 2 decimals)?}
steps: Identify: angle $= 60°$, opposite (tower height) $= 100$, want adjacent (ground distance).

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 65\text{-foot guy wire is anchored } 25 \text{ feet from the base of a tower.} \\ \text{What height on the tower is the wire attached to (round to 2 decimals)?}
steps: Identify: wire (hyp) $= 65$, ground distance (adj) $= 25$, want pole height (opp).
Two sides + right angle → Pythagorean theorem: $$h^2 + 25^2 = 65^2$$

questions.promptTex: \text{From a point on the ground } 50 \text{ meters from the base of a building,} \\ \text{the angle of elevation to the top of the building is } 38°. \\ \text{How tall is the building? (Use } \tan 38° \approx 0.781 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
steps: Sketch the picture: you stand 50 m from the building, look up at $38°$. The right triangle has horizontal leg (ground distance) $50$ and vertical leg (height) $h$.
Identify which sides: adjacent $= 50$, opposite $= h$. Two legs → **tangent**.

questions.promptTex: \text{A bird sits on top of a tree. From a point } 30 \text{ m away on the ground,} \\ \text{the angle of elevation to the bird is } 25°. \\ \text{How high above the ground is the bird?} \\ \text{(Use } \tan 25° \approx 0.466 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
steps: Sketch: ground distance $30$ m (adjacent), bird height $h$ (opposite), elevation angle $25°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{From the top of a } 75\text{-meter cliff, the angle of depression to a boat} \\ \text{at sea is } 22°. \text{ How far is the boat from the base of the cliff?} \\ \text{(Use } \tan 22° \approx 0.404 \text{; round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: The angle of depression from the top equals the angle of elevation from the boat (alternate interior angles).
steps: Sketch: cliff is vertical (height $75$), boat is on water some horizontal distance $d$ away. From the top of the cliff the line of sight goes down to the boat at $22°$ below horizontal.
By alternate interior angles, the angle of elevation from the boat back up to the cliff-top is also $22°$. This gives a right triangle with: opposite $= 75$, adjacent $= d$, angle $= 22°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{An airplane is flying at an altitude of } 2000 \text{ feet. The pilot spots} \\ \text{a runway with an angle of depression of } 15°. \\ \text{What is the horizontal distance from the plane to the runway?} \\ \text{(Use } \tan 15° \approx 0.268 \text{; round to nearest foot.)}
steps: Sketch: altitude $= 2000$ ft (opposite), horizontal distance to runway $= d$ (adjacent), depression angle $= 15°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 6\text{-foot person casts a shadow that is } 8 \text{ feet long.} \\ \text{What is the angle of elevation of the sun} \\ \text{(round to nearest 0.1°)?}
hints: Person's height = opposite = 6, shadow length = adjacent = 8.
steps: Sketch: person stands vertically (height $6$), shadow lies on the ground (length $8$). The sun's rays make an angle with the ground equal to the angle of elevation.
In the right triangle: opposite (person's height) $= 6$, adjacent (shadow) $= 8$, want the angle.

questions.promptTex: \text{From the top of a tower, the angle of depression to point A is } 30°. \\ \text{Point A is } 100 \text{ m from the base of the tower.} \\ \text{How tall is the tower?} \\ \text{(Use exact value of } \tan 30°; \text{ round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: Angle of depression at the top = angle of elevation at point A (both $30°$).
steps: Sketch: tower height $h$, ground distance $100$ to point A, angle of depression $30°$ from the top.
Equivalent angle of elevation from A is $30°$. Right triangle: opposite $= h$, adjacent $= 100$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the three-figure bearing of due East?}
hints: Three-figure bearings are measured clockwise from North.
Going 90° clockwise from North gets you to East.
steps: (Continuing: 180° = South, 270° = West, then back to 360°/000° = North.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Convert the compass bearing N 30° E to a three-figure bearing.}
hints: N 30° E means 30° east of due North.
Three-figure bearings start at North and count clockwise.
steps: N 30° E means **starting at North and rotating 30° toward East**.
Three-figure bearings also start at North and count clockwise.
Same starting point, same direction of rotation, same magnitude → bearing is **030°**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Convert the compass bearing S 45° W to a three-figure bearing.}
hints: Start at North (000°), go clockwise to South (180°), then continue 45° more toward West.
questions.explanation: S 45° W is 180° (south) + 45° (more clockwise toward west) = 225°.
steps: S 45° W means starting at South and rotating 45° toward West.
(Continuing past 270° would be north of due west; we stop at 225° in the SW.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A plane flies on a bearing of } 070° \text{ for } 200 \text{ km.} \\ \text{How far east of its starting point is it (round to 2 decimals)?} \\ \text{(Use } \sin 70° \approx 0.940 \text{)}
hints: East displacement = total distance × sin(bearing). Why? The east leg is opposite the angle from North.
steps: Sketch: North up, East right. The plane's path is at 070° (clockwise from North) — i.e., 70° east of due North.
Decompose the path into east and north components — a right triangle.
The east leg is **opposite** the 70° angle (measured at the start, between the path and the North axis).

questions.promptTex: \text{A boat sails } 12 \text{ km due East, then } 9 \text{ km due North.} \\ \text{How far is it from the starting point?}
hints: Two perpendicular legs → right triangle, Pythagorean theorem.
steps: East and North are perpendicular — the two legs form a right angle at the turning point.

questions.promptTex: \text{A hiker walks on a bearing of } 050° \text{ for } 4 \text{ km.} \\ \text{How far north of the start is she (round to 2 decimals)?} \\ \text{(Use } \cos 50° \approx 0.643 \text{)}
hints: North displacement = distance × cos(bearing). The north leg is adjacent to the bearing angle (measured from North).
steps: Sketch: hiker's path goes 050° (50° east of North).
The right triangle has hypotenuse 4 (path length), with the angle measured **from the North axis**.
North leg is **adjacent** to that angle, east leg is opposite.

questions.promptTex: \text{From a boat at sea, the angle of elevation to the top of an 80 m lighthouse is } 25°. \\ \text{How far is the boat from the base of the lighthouse? (Round to nearest meter.)} \\ \text{Use } \tan 25° \approx 0.466.
hints: The lighthouse is vertical; the sea line is horizontal — that's your right angle.
Opposite = 80 (lighthouse height). Adjacent = distance to base. Angle at the boat = 25°. Two legs → tangent.

steps.text: **Draw the scene.** The lighthouse rises vertically from the sea. The boat looks up at the top at 25°. The right angle is at the lighthouse's base where the wall meets the sea.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='130' width='260' height='40' fill='#dbeafe'/><line x1='0' y1='130' x2='260' y2='130' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='210' y='42' width='22' height='88' fill='#f8fafc' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='205' y='34' width='32' height='10' fill='#dc2626'/><circle cx='221' cy='30' r='3' fill='#fde68a'/><path d='M 30 132 L 70 132 L 64 142 L 36 142 Z' fill='#92400e' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='50' y1='132' x2='50' y2='112' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='50,112 68,128 50,128' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='52' y1='132' x2='210' y2='42' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='52' y1='132' x2='210' y2='132' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><line x1='210' y1='42' x2='210' y2='132' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><rect x='200' y='122' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 78 132 A 28 28 0 0 0 75 122' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='82' y='128' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>25°</text><text x='131' y='148' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>d = ?</text><text x='218' y='90' font-size='12'>80 m</text></svg>

steps.text: **Identify the sides.** From the boat's 25° angle: opposite = 80 (the lighthouse height), adjacent = $d$ (what we want). Two legs of the triangle — use **tangent**.
**Solve for $d$.** Multiply both sides by $d$, then divide by $\tan 25°$:

questions.promptTex: \text{A tree casts a 12 m shadow when the sun's angle of elevation is } 35°. \\ \text{How tall is the tree (round to 2 decimals)?} \\ \text{Use } \tan 35° \approx 0.700.
hints: Tree is vertical, shadow is horizontal — right angle at the tree's base.
Angle 35° is at the tip of the shadow (where the sun ray hits the ground). From there: opposite = tree height, adjacent = shadow length.

steps.text: **Set up the triangle.** Three pieces: the vertical tree, the horizontal shadow, and the sun ray slanting from sun to the top of the tree. The right angle sits where the tree meets the ground.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='0' y1='140' x2='260' y2='140' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='52' y='66' width='10' height='74' fill='#78350f'/><circle cx='57' cy='58' r='22' fill='#16a34a'/><line x1='62' y1='140' x2='198' y2='140' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='57' y1='36' x2='198' y2='140' stroke='#fde047' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='228' cy='62' r='14' fill='#fde047' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='220' y1='52' x2='234' y2='40' stroke='#fde047' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='240' y1='62' x2='256' y2='62' stroke='#fde047' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='234' y1='80' x2='246' y2='90' stroke='#fde047' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='62' y='130' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 180 140 A 24 24 0 0 0 178 124' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='168' y='134' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>35°</text><text x='130' y='156' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>12 m (shadow)</text><text x='14' y='100' font-size='12'>h = ?</text></svg>

steps.text: **Locate the 35° angle.** It's at the tip of the shadow (where the sun-ray hits the ground). From that vertex: opposite = $h$ (tree), adjacent = $12$ (shadow). Two legs → tangent.

questions.promptTex: \text{A 6 m wheelchair ramp rises at an angle of } 8° \text{ from the ground.} \\ \text{How high above the ground is the top of the ramp (round to 2 decimals)?} \\ \text{Use } \sin 8° \approx 0.139.
hints: The ramp itself is the hypotenuse (the long slanted edge). Height is the vertical leg.

steps.text: **Picture the ramp.** A slanted board, one end on the ground, the other end raised to height $h$. The board itself (6 m) is the hypotenuse; the vertical lift is the opposite side of the 8° angle.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='0' y1='120' x2='260' y2='120' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='30,120 220,120 220,90' fill='#fed7aa' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='220' y2='90' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.5'/><rect x='210' y='110' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 56 120 A 22 22 0 0 0 55 116' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='62' y='115' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>8°</text><text x='118' y='114' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>6 m</text><text x='228' y='108' font-size='12'>h = ?</text></svg>

steps.text: **Identify the sides.** From the 8° angle at the base: hypotenuse = 6 (ramp), opposite = $h$ (height). One side and hypotenuse → sine.
